beats.activity: Wake up, shower, coffee, and light breakfast at home.
beats.reasoning: Full energy start to the week; 35 min morning routine is typical for a 51-year-old ER nurse preparing for a shift.

beats.activity: Drive from Shoreline to Renton hospital for shift.
beats.reasoning: Commute from Shoreline to Renton is ~30–40 min; arriving by 8:15 gives buffer before typical ER shift start.

beats.activity: Work ER nursing shift at hospital.
beats.reasoning: 8-hour ER shift is standard; ends mid-afternoon, giving Logan time for social activity before evening.

beats.activity: Drive from Renton to downtown Seattle.
beats.reasoning: Heading downtown to catch the USA vs Wales FIFA match at a bar; Light Rail closure 9am–3pm doesn't affect this evening drive. ~35 min to Capitol Hill.

beats.activity: Grab an early dinner and settle in at a sports bar.
beats.reasoning: Logan values time with friends; arriving early to a downtown bar on a sunny Monday to watch the 11am match replay or catch pre-match coverage with fellow fans aligns with his social butterfly nature.

beats.activity: Watch USA vs Wales FIFA match and socialize with friends at the bar.
beats.reasoning: The 11am match likely has replays/coverage throughout the day; Logan catches it with friends post-shift—ideal outlet for a workaholic to decompress on a sunny Monday.

beats.activity: Drive from Capitol Hill back to Shoreline.
beats.reasoning: ~40 min drive north after social hour; arriving home by 9pm allows wind-down before bed.

beats.activity: Evening leisure at home: relax, light stretching, prepare gear for weekend camping.
beats.reasoning: Post-shift wind-down at home; planning weekend camping aligns with Logan's outdoor interests and gives him time to decompress before sleep.
8-hour sleep for recovery after ER shift; spans overnight, ending at 6:30am the next day as required.

diary: Started the day like any other Monday—coffee at home, then the drive down to Renton for my shift. The ER was steady but not brutal, which I'll take. A few traumas, the usual complaints, but nothing that kept me up mentally. By early afternoon I was already thinking about getting downtown. The weather was perfect, sunny and warm, so when I clocked out I decided to skip the usual commute home and head straight to Capitol Hill. Met up with some folks at the sports bar and grabbed an early dinner. The USA-Wales match was on, and honestly, it was the perfect excuse to decompress with a beer and some company. The energy was good—not too crazy since it was early. Watched the whole thing, cheered when we should've, groaned at the right moments. Drove home feeling loose for the first time all week. Back at the house, I stretched out, prepped my camping gear for the weekend. Ready for some time in the mountains.
thought_process: Work was fine but draining in that invisible way ER nursing is—you're present and sharp all day, then suddenly you're done and the adrenaline just evaporates. I could've gone straight home, but the weather was too good to waste, and honestly I needed the social reset. The light rail closure didn't matter since I was driving anyway. The match timing worked out perfectly; it gave me permission to linger downtown without it feeling aimless. My mood shifted somewhere between the hospital parking lot and the bar—from autopilot to actually present. Prepping for camping tonight felt like the final piece, a reminder that there's life outside the hospital walls. These little rituals matter when you work the hours I do.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
